statement.en: Do LLM Decoders Listen Fairly? Benchmarking How Language Model Priors Shape Bias in Speech Recognition
statement.summary: As pretrained large language models replace task-specific decoders in speech recognition, a critical question arises: do their text-derived priors make recognition fairer or more biased across demographic groups? We evaluate nine models spanning three architectural generations (CTC with no language model, encoder-decoder with an implicit LM, and LLM-based with an explicit pretrained decoder) on about 43,000 utterances across five demographic axes (ethnicity, accent, gender, age, first language) using Common Voice 24 and Meta's Fair-Speech, a controlled-prompt dataset that eliminates vocabulary confounds. On clean audio, three findings challenge assumptions: LLM decoders do not amplify racial bias (Granite-8B has the best ethnicity fairness, max/min WER = 2.28); Whisper exhibits pathologica

proposer: Srishti Ginjala, Eric Fosler-Lussier, Christopher W. Myers et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: Empirical paper presenting novel benchmarking results on a live research frontier (LLM decoder fairness in ASR). Claims are falsifiable and grounded in experimental design, but the field is rapidly evolving (2026 date, post-Whisper variants emerging). Results challenge prior assumptions but require replication across newer model generations and datasets. Status: validated conference/preprint work, not yet consensus-canonical.
